name: BMW M3
reliability: 6/10 — thrilling but high-strung; every generation has a signature expensive failure
popular_mods: ECU tune — huge gains on turbo S55/S58 (500+ whp easily achievable)
Downpipes, intake, charge pipe, upgraded intercooler (S55/S58)
NA cars (S54/S65): headers + tune, or supercharger for big power
Coilovers, sway bars, big brake kit
Preventive crank hub fix (F80) / rod bearing refresh (E46/E90)
oil_interval: Every 5,000-7,500 miles (BMW's 10k+ interval is too long for these engines)

generations.E46 (2000–2006): 3.2L S54 inline-6, 333hp, 8,000rpm redline. A high point of the breed. 6-speed manual or clunky SMG. Watch rod bearings and rear subframe cracking.
generations.E90/E92/E93 (2008–2013): 4.0L S65 V8, 414hp, 8,400rpm redline — the only V8 M3. Sedan/coupe/vert. Rod bearings and throttle actuators are the weak spots.
generations.F80 (2014–2018): 3.0L S55 twin-turbo inline-6, 425hp (Competition 444hp). First turbo M3 — torque leapt to 406 lb-ft. Infamous crank hub slip.
generations.G80 (2021–present): 3.0L S58 twin-turbo inline-6, 473hp / 503hp Competition. Added xDrive AWD. Controversial oversized grille. Manual still offered on base RWD.

common_issues: Rod bearing failure (E46 S54, E90 S65) — factory clearances too tight; preventive replacement advised
Crank hub slipping (F80 S55) — secured by a single friction bolt
maintenance_tips: Budget for preventive rod bearing replacement on E46/E90
Install a crank hub fix on F80 before tuning for higher power
Shorten oil intervals well below BMW's recommendation
Inspect E46 rear subframe for cracks at the chassis mounts
